section.title: Achan's Sin
section.sectionRationale: After the triumph of Jericho comes a shocking defeat. One man, Achan, has secretly taken what was devoted to the LORD, and because of that hidden sin all Israel is defeated at little Ai. God exposes the sin, Achan is identified and confesses, and judgment falls. It is one movement, the tragedy of Achan, which teaches the deadly seriousness of sin, the corporate nature of the covenant community, and the holiness of the God who dwells among His people.
section.thesis: Sin is never merely private: one man's hidden theft of what belonged to God brings defeat and death on the whole community, revealing that the LORD who fights for Israel is holy, that His people are bound together, and that unconfessed sin must be exposed and dealt with before God will again be with them.
section.frameNote: Four beats: the hidden sin and the defeat at Ai, verses 1 to 5; Joshua's anguished prayer and God's answer, verses 6 to 15; the exposing of Achan, verses 16 to 21; and the judgment in the Valley of Achor, verses 22 to 26.

units.label: The hidden sin and the defeat at Ai
units.note: the anger of the LORD burned against Israel

verses.text: The Israelites, however, acted {{maal|unfaithfully}} regarding the things devoted to destruction. Achan son of Carmi, the son of Zabdi, the son of Zerah, of the tribe of Judah, took some of what was set apart. So the anger of the LORD burned against the Israelites.
Meanwhile, Joshua sent men from Jericho to Ai, which is near Beth-aven to the east of Bethel, and told them, “Go up and spy out the land.” So the men went up and spied out Ai.

On returning to Joshua, they reported, “There is no need to send all the people; two or three thousand men are enough to go up and attack Ai. Since the people of Ai are so few, you need not wear out all our people there.”
So about three thousand men went up, but they fled before the men of Ai.

And the men of Ai struck down about thirty-six of them, chasing them from the gate as far as the quarries and striking them down on the slopes. So the hearts of the people melted and became like water.

units.label: Joshua's prayer and God's answer
units.note: Israel has sinned; you cannot stand until you remove it

verses.text: Then Joshua tore his clothes and fell facedown before the ark of the LORD until evening, as did the elders of Israel; and they all sprinkled dust on their heads.

“O, Lord GOD,” Joshua said, “why did You ever bring this people across the Jordan to deliver us into the hand of the Amorites to be destroyed? If only we had been content to stay on the other side of the Jordan!
O Lord, what can I say, now that Israel has turned its back and run from its enemies?
When the Canaanites and all who live in the land hear about this, they will surround us and wipe out our name from the earth. Then what will You do for Your great name?”
But the LORD said to Joshua, “Stand up! Why have you fallen on your face?

Israel has sinned; they have transgressed My covenant that I commanded them, and they have taken some of what was {{cherem|devoted to destruction}}. Indeed, they have stolen and lied, and they have put these things with their own possessions.

This is why the Israelites cannot stand against their enemies. They will turn their backs and run from their enemies, because they themselves have been set apart for destruction. I will no longer be with you unless you remove from among you whatever is devoted to destruction.

Get up and consecrate the people, saying, ‘Consecrate yourselves for tomorrow, for this is what the LORD, the God of Israel, says: Among you, O Israel, there are things devoted to destruction. You cannot stand against your enemies until you remove them.

In the morning you must present yourselves tribe by tribe. The tribe that the LORD selects shall come forward clan by clan, and the clan that the LORD selects shall come forward family by family, and the family that the LORD selects shall come forward man by man.

The one who is caught with the things devoted to destruction must be burned, along with all that belongs to him, because he has transgressed the covenant of the LORD and committed an outrage in Israel.’”

units.label: The exposing of Achan
units.note: my son, give glory to the LORD and confess

verses.text: So Joshua arose early the next morning and had Israel come forward tribe by tribe, and the tribe of Judah was selected.
He had the clans of Judah come forward, and the clan of the Zerahites was selected. He had the clan of the Zerahites come forward, and the family of Zabdi was selected.
And he had the family of Zabdi come forward man by man, and Achan son of Carmi, the son of Zabdi, the son of Zerah, of the tribe of Judah, was selected.
So Joshua said to Achan, “My son, give glory to the LORD, the God of Israel, and make a confession to Him. I urge you to tell me what you have done; do not hide it from me.”
“It is true,” Achan replied, “I have sinned against the LORD, the God of Israel. This is what I did:

When I saw among the spoils a beautiful cloak from Shinar, two hundred shekels of silver, and a bar of gold weighing fifty shekels, I {{chamad|coveted}} them and took them. They are hidden in the ground inside my tent, with the silver underneath.”

units.label: Judgment in the Valley of Achor
units.note: why have you brought trouble on us; the LORD turned from His anger

verses.text: So Joshua sent messengers who ran to the tent, and there it all was, hidden in his tent, with the silver underneath.
They took the things from inside the tent, brought them to Joshua and all the Israelites, and spread them out before the LORD.

Then Joshua, together with all Israel, took Achan son of Zerah, the silver, the cloak, the bar of gold, his sons and daughters, his oxen and donkeys and sheep, his tent, and everything else he owned, and brought them to the Valley of Achor.
“Why have you brought this trouble upon us?” said Joshua. “Today the LORD will bring trouble upon you!” And all Israel stoned him to death. Then they stoned the others and burned their bodies.
And they heaped over Achan a large pile of rocks that remains to this day. So the LORD turned from His burning anger. Therefore that place is called the Valley of Achor to this day.

maal.gloss: To act unfaithfully, to commit a treacherous breach of trust, especially against holy things or against God. Achan's theft is not merely stealing; it is maal, a betrayal of the covenant, a sacrilege against what belonged wholly to the LORD. The word frames the sin as a violation of the relationship between God and His people, which is why it defiles the whole nation.

cherem.gloss: That which is devoted irrevocably to the LORD, set apart for destruction or for His treasury. Jericho and its spoil were placed under the cherem; by taking some for himself, Achan makes himself and, through the covenant bond, the camp of Israel liable to the very ban he violated, so that Israel too becomes set apart for destruction until the devoted thing is removed.

chamad.gloss: To covet, desire, take pleasure in wanting. Achan traces his sin to its root: I saw, I coveted, I took, the very sequence of the fall in Eden and a direct breach of the tenth commandment. The word exposes the inner spring of the sacrilege, showing that the outward theft grew from a heart that desired what God had forbidden.

basic.context: Right after the great victory at Jericho, Israel suffers a humiliating defeat at the small town of Ai, and thirty-six men die. Joshua is devastated and falls before God in prayer. But God tells him the problem is not with God; it is sin in the camp. One man, Achan, had secretly taken some of the plunder from Jericho that had been devoted wholly to the LORD, and hidden it in his tent. Because Israel is one covenant people, that hidden sin brought defeat on everyone. God has the nation brought forward tribe by tribe until Achan is singled out. He confesses that he saw, coveted, and took the forbidden things. Judgment falls in the Valley of Achor, and God's anger is turned away. The question at the heart of it is: why does God take sin among His people so seriously?
basic.hermeneutics: This is historical narrative, and it is one of the hardest chapters in Joshua because Achan's household shares in the judgment. It must be read within its own covenant setting, where the herem was in force and the whole camp had become liable, and not turned into a rule for how communities punish sin today. What endures is its clear teaching: sin is deadly serious, it is never merely private, and God is holy and dwells among His people, so unconfessed sin cannot simply be ignored.
basic.originalLanguages: Achan's act is maal, an unfaithful, treacherous breach against holy things. He took some of the cherem, what was devoted wholly to God. And he names the root: he saw and chamad, coveted, and took, the pattern of Eden and the breach of the tenth commandment.
basic.typology: The Valley of Achor, the place of trouble and judgment, is later promised as a door of hope, Hosea 2:15, pointing to how God turns the place of judgment into a place of renewed hope, ultimately in Christ. Achan bearing judgment for sin against the community throws into relief the one who bears judgment for others to save them.
basic.christ: Achan sinned and brought trouble on the people, and he died for his own sin; Jesus, though sinless, took the trouble of His people upon Himself and died in their place, turning away God's anger not from one man but for all who believe. The Valley of Achor, trouble turned to hope, points to the gospel, where the place of judgment becomes a door of hope in Christ.
basic.god: God is holy and cannot be treated lightly; His anger burns against sacrilege. He is the God of the covenant who binds His people together, so that sin among them is everyone's concern. Yet He is also gracious: He does not abandon Israel but shows them the way back through confession and the removal of sin, and He turns from His anger once the sin is dealt with.
basic.crossReferences: Exodus 20 forbids coveting, the root of Achan's sin. Numbers 32 warns that sin will find you out. Hosea 2 turns the Valley of Achor into a door of hope. And 1 Corinthians 5 shows the New Testament church removing sin from among them so the whole body is not defiled.
basic.oneStory: The joy of Jericho gives way to the tragedy of Achan, showing that the greatest threat to God's people is not the enemy outside but sin within. God exposes and removes the sin so He can again be with His people. The Valley of Achor, trouble turned to hope, points forward to Christ, who bears the trouble of His people and opens a door of hope.

context.literary: Chapter 7 is the dark counterpart to chapter 6: the triumph of Jericho is followed at once by the defeat at Ai, and the reason is announced in the very first verse, before the reader even learns of the defeat, so that we watch the disaster unfold knowing its hidden cause. The narrowing search, tribe, clan, family, man, builds suspense and underscores that nothing is hidden from God. The naming of the Valley of Achor, trouble, and Achan, troubler, ties the man to the place and the theme. The chapter sets up chapter 8, where Ai is finally taken once the sin is removed.
context.historical: Ai was a small town in the hill country near Bethel, so its defeat of Israel was shocking. The herem on Jericho meant its spoil belonged wholly to God, unlike later cities where plunder was permitted, so Achan's theft was a direct sacrilege. The cloak from Shinar, that is, from Babylonia, was a luxury import; the silver and gold were substantial weights. Covenant solidarity, whereby a community shared in the guilt and consequences of a member's sin, was assumed in Israel's world. Stoning and burning were forms of covenant judgment for grave offenses, and the heap of stones marked the place as a lasting warning.
context.canonical: The account teaches the corporate seriousness of sin, a theme the New Testament carries forward when Paul warns that a little leaven leavens the whole lump and calls the church to purge sin from among them, 1 Corinthians 5. Achan's confession, I saw, I coveted, I took, deliberately echoes Eve in Eden, tying his sin to the fall. Most strikingly, Hosea later takes the Valley of Achor, this place of judgment, and promises to make it a door of hope, a redemptive reversal that points toward the gospel, where the place of trouble becomes the place of grace through Christ.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To show that hidden sin in the covenant community brings defeat and cannot be ignored, to reveal the holiness of the God who dwells among His people, to teach that sin is never merely private, and to trace the way back to God's favor through the exposure, confession, and removal of the devoted thing.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The chapter describes a specific covenant judgment under the herem, including the hard inclusion of Achan's household; it does not prescribe such penalties for any later community, and the New Testament deals with sin in the church through discipline and restoration, not stoning. What is prescriptive is the enduring truth it dramatizes: God is holy, sin is deadly and corporate in its effects, and it must be honestly confronted, confessed, and removed.

debates.question: Why were Achan's sons and daughters caught up in the judgment?

views.label: Covenant solidarity and likely complicity
views.case: In Israel's covenant world the household was a single unit, and the stolen goods were buried in the family tent, which strongly suggests the family knew and concealed the sin; the judgment falls on a household implicated in hiding what was devoted to God, not on innocents ignorant of the crime.

views.label: A severe covenant sign of sin's corporate reach
views.case: Whatever the family's precise knowledge, the passage dramatizes in the starkest terms that sin is never contained to the individual; its consequences spread outward through the community, a sobering picture the text presents as covenant judgment in a unique moment, not as a model, and one that Deuteronomy 24:16 elsewhere limits by forbidding executing children for a father's sin.

views.label: Read in tension, awaiting the gospel resolution
views.case: Scripture holds together both the corporate reality of sin and the principle of individual responsibility, and this hard episode is not the final word; it presses toward the cross, where One bears the sin of many so that the guilty need not perish, resolving the tension the chapter exposes.

debates.bearing: However one weighs the family's complicity, the passage's abiding point is the terrible seriousness and corporate reach of sin against a holy God, and it drives the reader to long for the substitute who bears sin so others may live.

study.originalLanguages: The chapter names the sin with precise and weighty words. Achan's act is called maal, a treacherous breach of faith, the word used for sacrilege against holy things and betrayal of God Himself; his was not petty theft but covenant treachery, which is why it defiles the whole nation. What he took was part of the cherem, the plunder of Jericho devoted irrevocably to the LORD; by grasping it he transfers the ban to himself and, through the bond of the covenant, to all Israel, so that the people who carried the ban now fall under it, unable to stand against their enemies until the devoted thing is purged. And Achan traces the sin to its root with three verbs that echo Eden: I saw, I chamad, I coveted, and I took, Genesis 3:6, the very shape of the fall and a direct breach of the last commandment, you shall not covet, Exodus 20:17. The vocabulary lays the sin bare: a treacherous grasp at what belonged to God, sprung from a coveting heart, that brought the whole community under judgment.

typology.type: The Valley of Achor, the place of trouble and judgment
typology.fulfillment: Turned into a door of hope, a redemptive reversal pointing to the grace God brings out of judgment in Christ, Hosea 2:15 and Romans 5:20.

typology.type: Achan bearing judgment so God's anger turns from Israel
typology.fulfillment: By contrast, Christ, though guiltless, bearing His people's judgment so God's wrath turns from them, 2 Corinthians 5:21 and Isaiah 53:5-6.

typology.type: The exposure of hidden sin before the holy God
typology.fulfillment: The final disclosure of all hidden things and the call now to walk in the light, Luke 12:2-3 and 1 John 1:7-9.

study.christ: Achan's story is a dark mirror that makes the gospel shine. He sinned, he was exposed, and he bore the trouble he had caused, dying for his own guilt so that God's anger would turn from Israel. The Lord Jesus reverses every part of that. He was without sin, yet He took upon Himself the trouble of His people; He was made sin for us, though He knew no sin, so that in Him we might become the righteousness of God, 2 Corinthians 5:21. Where Achan's death turned away wrath from a nation for a time, Christ's death turns away wrath from all who believe, forever. And the Valley of Achor itself preaches Christ: Hosea takes this very valley of judgment and promises that God will make it a door of hope, Hosea 2:15, exactly what God does at the cross, transforming the place of trouble and death into the doorway of grace and life.
study.god: This chapter reveals the holiness of God with unsettling clarity. He dwells among His people, and because He is holy, sacrilege in the camp is intolerable; His anger burns, and He will not go out with Israel's armies while devoted things lie hidden in a tent. He is the God of the covenant, who binds His people into one body so that no sin is merely private and no member sins alone. Yet He is not only severe. He does not abandon Israel to defeat but graciously shows them the way back, through consecration, confession, and the removal of the sin, and once it is dealt with, He turns from His burning anger. The God of Achan's valley is holy, just, and jealous for His people's purity, and also merciful, opening a path from judgment back to His presence, a path that runs at last to the cross.

crossReferences.note: You shall not covet; the tenth commandment names the very root Achan confesses, I coveted them and took them.
Be sure your sin will find you out; Achan's hidden theft is exposed before the holy God.
God promises to make the Valley of Achor a door of hope, turning the place of judgment into grace.
A little leaven leavens the whole lump; the church, like Israel, must remove sin from among them.
God made the sinless Christ to be sin for us, the reverse of Achan, so that we might become the righteousness of God.

study.oneStory: The exhilaration of Jericho collapses into the tragedy of Achan, teaching that the deadliest threat to God's people is not the enemy outside but sin within. God exposes and removes the hidden sin so that He can again go out with His people, and He turns from His anger. The Valley of Achor, trouble, becomes in Hosea a door of hope, and both point forward to Christ, who bears the trouble of His people, turns away the wrath of God, and opens the true and lasting door of hope for all who believe.

quiz.q: Why was Israel defeated at Ai?

options.text: Because Achan had secretly taken things devoted to the LORD, and his hidden sin brought guilt on the whole nation.
options.note: Verses 1 and 11 to 12 name Achan's sin as the cause of the defeat.

options.text: Because the army of Ai was far larger and stronger than Israel expected.
options.note: Ai was actually small; the true cause was sin in the camp, not the enemy's strength.

options.text: Because Joshua failed to pray before the battle.
options.note: The problem was not Joshua's prayer but Achan's hidden sin; God said, Israel has sinned.

options.text: Because God had abandoned His covenant with Israel for good.
options.note: God had not abandoned the covenant; He showed Israel the way back once the sin was removed.

quiz.q: How did Achan describe the way his sin unfolded?

options.text: He saw the plunder, coveted it, and took it, hiding it in his tent.
options.note: Verse 21 traces the sin from seeing to coveting to taking, echoing Eden.

options.text: He took the goods by accident, not realizing they were forbidden.
options.note: It was no accident; he confessed he saw, coveted, and deliberately took and hid them.

options.text: He was ordered by another Israelite to take them.
options.note: No one ordered him; he confessed the coveting was his own.

options.text: He never actually took anything and was falsely accused.
options.note: He confessed the theft, and the goods were found hidden in his tent.

quiz.q: What did God require before He would again be with Israel?

options.text: That they consecrate themselves and remove the devoted thing from among them.
options.note: Verses 12 to 13 make removing the sin the condition of God's renewed presence.

options.text: That they offer a larger army for the next attack on Ai.
options.note: The issue was not army size but removing the sin; a bigger army could not fix a holiness problem.

options.text: That they pay a fine to the town of Ai.
options.note: No payment to Ai was involved; God required the devoted thing be removed from the camp.

options.text: That Joshua step down as leader.
options.note: God told Joshua to stand up and act; the remedy was dealing with sin, not replacing the leader.

quiz.q: What does the tragedy of Achan teach about sin?

options.text: Sin against a holy God is deadly serious and never merely private; it affects the whole community.
options.note: One man's hidden sin brought defeat and death on all Israel, showing sin's corporate reach.

options.text: Sin only matters if it is public and obvious.
options.note: The opposite: Achan's sin was hidden, yet it defiled the whole camp; hidden sin is still deadly.

options.text: Personal sin has no effect on anyone else.
options.note: The chapter shows sin is corporate in its effects; it is never merely private.

options.text: God does not really care about small acts of disobedience.
options.note: God's anger burned over this sin; He takes even hidden disobedience with utter seriousness.

quiz.q: How does the Valley of Achor point forward in Scripture?

options.text: Hosea makes this place of trouble a door of hope, pointing to how God brings grace out of judgment in Christ.
options.note: Hosea 2:15 turns Achor into hope, foreshadowing the gospel reversal at the cross.

options.text: It shows that God's judgment can never be reversed or redeemed.
options.note: The opposite: God later turns this very valley of judgment into a door of hope.

options.text: It teaches that God's people should copy Achan's punishment today.
options.note: The specific covenant judgment is not a model; the church deals with sin through discipline and restoration.

options.text: It has no bearing on the rest of the Bible's story.
options.note: Hosea and the gospel pick up the valley's theme; it points to grace out of judgment in Christ.

questions: Achan's sin was hidden, yet it brought real harm to everyone around him. Where might hidden sin in your life be affecting others more than you realize?
Achan traced his fall to seeing, coveting, and taking. Where do you need to guard your heart against coveting what God has said is not yours?
God would not overlook the sin, but He did show Israel the way back through confession and removal. Is there something you need to bring honestly into the light before God rather than hiding it?
The place of trouble became a door of hope. How does it encourage you that God can bring grace and renewal even out of your worst failures when you turn to Him?
Achan died for his own sin, but Christ bore the trouble of His people to turn God's anger away. How does resting in Christ's substitution free you to confess your sin honestly rather than fearfully?

translationNotes.note: Acted unfaithfully renders maal, a treacherous breach of trust against holy things; the things devoted to destruction render forms of the Hebrew cherem, as the BSB footnote notes, recurring in verses 11, 12, 13, and 15.
Achan means troubler, as the BSB footnote notes; he is also called Achar in 1 Chronicles 2:7, tying his name to the trouble he caused.
The cloak from Shinar is a luxury import from Babylonia, as the BSB footnote notes; the silver and gold are given in shekel weights the footnotes convert to modern measures.
The Valley of Achor means trouble, as the BSB footnote notes, tying the place to Achan the troubler and to Joshua's word about trouble.
